topic: winter prep — stacking firewood, woodstove check

turns.content: spent the whole morning stacking firewood and now my arms are telling me i'm not 25 anymore. you know how it is
Ha, firewood has a way of reminding you of every year you've earned. How much did you get stacked? Are you set for the winter or still building the pile?

got about 3 cords stacked along the south side of the house. need at least 5 to get through to march. Raymond's supposed to split the rest this weekend but we'll see. he always finds something else to do in the workshop
3 out of 5 cords is solid progress though. You might need to hide his tools until the wood's done. Is this pinon or juniper you're burning, or a mix?
mostly juniper. some pinon. the pinon smells better but the juniper burns longer and hotter. you mix them, you know. pinon to start because it catches easy, juniper to hold the heat through the night
That's a good system — the pinon for ignition and aroma, the juniper for sustained heat. Have you checked the woodstove and chimney yet, or is that still on the list?

that's what i need to do this weekend. last year i waited too long and when i lit the first fire the whole house filled with smoke. the chimney was packed with creosote. had to air the house out in 30 degree weather. Raymond just stood outside laughing. very helpful

Oh no — a house full of smoke in freezing temps is not fun. Creosote buildup is no joke either, that's a chimney fire waiting to happen. Do you clean it yourself or hire someone? A good chimney brush kit pays for itself pretty fast.

i do it myself. got the brush and rods from the hardware store in Gallup a few years back. it's not hard, just messy. you go up on the roof and push the brush down. wear clothes you don't care about. the soot gets everywhere

Messy but necessary. While you're at it, check the stove gaskets and the door seal too. If the door seal is worn out you lose a lot of heat and the stove burns through wood faster. A new gasket rope is cheap and easy to replace.

good point. the door doesn't close as tight as it used to. i noticed last spring but then it was warm and i forgot about it. i'll check the gasket when i clean the chimney. probably need to replace it

If it's not sealing tight, you're definitely due for a new gasket. Most hardware stores carry the high-temp gasket rope and cement. Just peel out the old one, clean the channel, and press in the new one. Takes about 20 minutes and you'll notice the difference right away in how well the stove holds heat overnight.
i'll add it to the list. chimney sweep, gasket, finish stacking. and somehow convince Raymond to actually split the rest of the wood instead of making earrings. you know priorities

Ha — tell him the earrings won't keep the house warm in January. You've got a good list. Tackle the chimney first since you need dry weather for the roof, then the gasket while the stove's cold. The wood can wait for Raymond's guilty conscience to kick in.
his guilty conscience. that's a good one. alright i'm going to go take some ibuprofen and stare at the woodpile from the porch. ahéhee'
You earned that porch sit. Rest up and hit the chimney this weekend. Stay warm out there.
